issue_title: Request using mutual auth returns 200 but raises error
issue_body:
With `0.11.0` (`requests == 2.14.1`, `pykerberos == 1.1.14`):

```python
requests.get('https://SERVER', verify=False, auth=HTTPKerberosAuth())
```

Raises:

```python
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<ipython-input-39-a3f889e087c4>", line 1, in <module>
    requests.get('https://SERVER', verify=False, auth=HTTPKerberosAuth(mutual_authentication=1))
  File "/home/musttu/Code/virtualenvs/cosmos/lib/python3.4/site-packages/requests/api.py", line 72, in get
    return request('get', url, params=params, **kwargs)
  File "/home/musttu/Code/virtualenvs/cosmos/lib/python3.4/site-packages/requests/api.py", line 58, in request
    return session.request(method=method, url=url, **kwargs)
  File "/home/musttu/Code/virtualenvs/cosmos/lib/python3.4/site-packages/requests/sessions.py", line 518, in request
    resp = self.send(prep, **send_kwargs)
  File "/home/musttu/Code/virtualenvs/cosmos/lib/python3.4/site-packages/requests/sessions.py", line 646, in send
    r = dispatch_hook('response', hooks, r, **kwargs)
  File "/home/musttu/Code/virtualenvs/cosmos/lib/python3.4/site-packages/requests/hooks.py", line 31, in dispatch_hook
    _hook_data = hook(hook_data, **kwargs)
  File "/home/musttu/Code/virtualenvs/cosmos/lib/python3.4/site-packages/requests_kerberos/kerberos_.py", line 287, in handle_response
    return self.handle_response(_r, num_401s=num_401s, **kwargs)
  File "/home/musttu/Code/virtualenvs/cosmos/lib/python3.4/site-packages/requests_kerberos/kerberos_.py", line 294, in handle_response
    _r = self.handle_other(response)
  File "/home/musttu/Code/virtualenvs/cosmos/lib/python3.4/site-packages/requests_kerberos/kerberos_.py", line 217, in handle_other
    "{0}".format(response))
MutualAuthenticationError: Unable to authenticate <Response [200]>
```

That looks weird - the server returned `200` but it is reported as a failure?

I stepped some code, and response from the server is indeed correct (containing expected response body etc.).

Enabling logging gives:

```python
ERROR:requests_kerberos.kerberos_:authenticate_server(): authGSSClientStep() failed:
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/musttu/Code/virtualenvs/cosmos/lib/python3.4/site-packages/requests_kerberos/kerberos_.py", line 258, in authenticate_server
    result = kerberos.authGSSClientStep(self.context[host],
kerberos.GSSError: (('Invalid token was supplied', 589824), ('Success', 100001))
ERROR:requests_kerberos.kerberos_:handle_other(): Mutual authentication failed
```

Setting `force_preemptive=True` makes no difference.

Setting `mutual_authentication=DISABLE` makes the call succeed... but shouldn't it work also with enabled?

The target server is Jenkins, if that matters.

Current plan of attack:

- [x] Remove `--plugin=protoc-gen-grpc=$(GRPC_PLUGIN)` from "helper" modules that don't use gRPC (e.g. `google.bigtable.v1.bigtable_data`) (this way sub-packages that don't need gRPC can use them) (#1354)
- [x] Ditch shared generated modules for those included with [`protobuf`][6] and [`googleapis-common-protos`][7] (#1353) ~~Move shared (i.e. non-Bigtable specific) modules from `gcloud.bigtable._generated` to `gcloud._generated` (this way all sub-packages can have access to shared protobuf message classes) and then subsequently update the `bigtable` imports to accommodate this change~~
- [x] Pull out [`_get_pb_property_value`][5] in `gcloud.bigtable.cluster` into core (`gcloud._helpers`) and separate out a `_has_field` piece since `pb_message.HasField` doesn't work on non-message fields in `proto3` (#1329)
- [x] Update `Makefile` to reflect changes above (i.e. make sure it can be run without changing the code) (See #1316)
- [x] Update `Makefile` to automatically add the `_foo.proto` files to the repo (See #1317)
- [x] Fold `Batch.add_auto_id_entity` into `Batch.put` (the `auto_id_entity` mutation is removed in favor of just using `insert` with a partial key) (see #1296)
- [x] Loosen `Batch` implementation's reliance on `Mutation` message class (it is used in a `LookupRequest` and their combined structure changes in `v1beta3`) (See #1306, #1319, #1341)
- [x] Remove use of `serializable` in `Client.transaction` and `Transaction` (it is no longer an option in `v1beta3`, so we will curtail it's use in `v1beta2` before the switch) (See #1294)
- [x] Manually partition the `_datastore_v1_pb2.py` module into 3 modules that just import portions of the namespace. These modules (`datastore_pb2.py`, `entity_pb2.py`, `query_pb2.py`) will be according to the membership in the new [proto definitions][4] for `v1beta3`. (See #1297, #1299, #1301, #1328)
- [x] Switch over imports from directly using `_datastore_v1_pb2.py` to using our "shim" imports to mock the structure of `v1beta3`. (See #1297, #1299, #1301, #1328)
- [x] Make sure `API_BASE_URL` for the `Connection` class is used (rather than a parent's version) (See #1293)
- [x] Change `Client.dataset_id` to `Client.project` (in advance of the rename to `PartitionId.project_id` in `v1beta3`) (#1330)
- [x] Change the return type of `Connection.commit` to be a tuple of `index_updates` and `mutation_results` (they were previously on the same result object but are being split apart in `v1beta3`) (See #1314)
- [x] Replace uses of `HasField` for non-message values with `_has_field` (see above) (#1329)
- [x] Remove `v1beta2` generated `pb2` file and old `.proto` definition (can also remove `_datastore_v1_pb2.py` from the `pylintrc_default` ignored file) and delete our "shim" modules (#1355, #1428)
- [x] Update `Makefile` to incorporate protobuf definitions for `v1beta3` (I did this in a [side-project][3], #1353, #1354, #1355, #1428)
- [x] ~~Rewrite our "shim" imports to use the actual `datastore._generated` files for `v1beta3`.~~
- [x] Switch `API_BASE_URL` from `https://www.googleapis.com` to `https://datastore.googleapis.com` and drop `'https://www.googleapis.com/auth/userinfo.email` from the scope list (#1339, #1406)
- [x] Accommodate renames / retyped / removed
    - [x] `CommitRequest.mutation  -->  CommitRequest.mutations` (#1461)
    - [x] `LookupRequest.key  -->  LookupRequest.keys` (~~#1358~~, #1456)
    - [x] `AllocateIdsResponse.key  -->  AllocateIdsResponse.keys` (~~#1358~~, #1456)
    - [x] `AllocateIdsRequest.key  -->  AllocateIdsRequest.keys` (~~#1358~~, #1456)
    - [x] `Key.path_element  -->  Key.path` (~~#1360~~, #1457)
    - [x] `Entity.property  -->  Entity.properties` (#1458)
    - [x] `Query.group_by  -->  Query.distinct_on` (~~#1357~~, #1455)
    - [x] `Query.limit  -->  Query.limit.value` (~~#1357~~, #1455)
    - [x] `QueryResultBatch.entity_result  -->  QueryResultBatch.entity_results` (~~#1357~~, #1455)
    - [x] `PartitionId.namespace  -->  PartitionId.namespace_id` (~~#1359~~, #1452)
    - [x] `PartitionId.dataset_id  -->  PartitionId.project_id` (~~#1359~~, #1452)
    - [x] `Value.indexed  -->  Value.exclude_from_indexes` (~~#1365~~, #1453)
    - [x] `Value.list_value  -->  Value.array_value` (#1460)
    - [x] `Value.null_value` added (#1464)
    - [x] `Value.timestamp_microseconds_value  -->  Value.timestamp_value` (with type change) (#1361, #1459)
    - [x] `Value.geo_point_value` added (#1464)
    - [x] `Value.blob_key_value` removed
    - [x] `CompositeFilter.filter  -->  CompositeFilter.filters` (~~#1356~~, #1454)
    - [x] `CompositeFilter.operation  -->  CompositeFilter.op` (~~#1356~~, #1454)

issue_title: Documentation Issue: Environment Variables Section is Not Complete
issue_body: The [section in the documentation that describes environment variables](https://googlecloudplatform.github.io/google-cloud-python/stable/google-cloud-api.html#module-google.cloud.environment_vars) does not list all of them. In particular, it misses all of the environment variables defined in `google.auth.environment_vars` ([file here](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-auth-library-python/blob/master/google/auth/environment_vars.py)). Even though this is a separate project, it is packaged with and relied upon by google-cloud-python. These settings are pretty important.

issue_title: CloudFront TTL issue - undocumented AWS change
issue_body:
Suddenly working with CloudFront via CloudFormation if you pass in a MinTTL value for your cache behavior, you will get an error saying:

**MinTTL must be less equal to DefaultTTL must be less equal to MaxTTL**

however, according to the docs (as of 12/21) there are no DefaultTTL or MaxTTL values: http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSCloudFormation/latest/UserGuide/aws-properties-cloudfront-defaultcachebehavior.html#cfn-cloudfront-defaultcachebehavior-minttl

Troposphere reflects this fact and won't let you add MaxTTL or DefaultTTL.  However, now all CloudFront adjustments using TTLs will fail.  If you manually add in the MaxTTL and DefaultTTL values to the code, now it works!  Just verified and tested working with adjusted code.  PR to fix issue is #360

Troposphere is awesome! Thank you everyone for working on it.

I was able to create a Role with title value containing "-" i.e. alphanumeric. This passes troposphere validation, but fails when running the template. Has anyone else seen this behavior?

It would be nice if PEX could support '-m {package}'.

There are a lot of packages that do [sys.executable, '-m', 'something-else'].  Currently there isn't really a way to embed these into a PEX.

Specifically, pip calls setuptools in this fashion.

pex can build a runnable version of itself right?  does the self-hosted pex also carry along a version of pip, or does it depend on the system having a pip version available?

I am working on a project that wants to self-embed its build tools as part of the published pex artifact.  That means pex + pip + virtualenv embedded into the pex.  It isn't blocked by `python -m`, just wanted to call out that having that support would make this kind of project a lot easier.

issue_title: Jar publish relies on --jvm-options to be set.
issue_body:
There are several tasks across the codebase that positively require certain options to be set. This is likely uncaught by core developers because we have many of these flags set in our pants.ini. It only show up when someone tries a task that org doesn't have options set for.

This example is in jar_publish, which copies jvm_options and appends to it. If jvm_options are not set in the publish scope, an error (AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'append') comes from line 597. Another I have seen (but not tracked down) is using len() checks on options, expecting them to be some specific number but not considering it might be unset.

issue_title: build_invalidator and patns cache mismatch causes classes to not get written to pants
issue_body:
Affects 1.3.0 (maybe more).
Requirements: JVM project with zinc (see https://github.com/jsirois/lf-example for example) with multiple branches affecting the same class (in this case Example class)

1.  Start off in branch A.  Do a ./pants compile ::.  See the Example class appear in the cache directory
2.  Switch to branch B.  Do a ./pants compile ::.  See both versions of the Example class in the cache directory
3.  Delete the cache directory for just the Example class
4.  Switch back to branch A.  Do a ./pants compile ::.  Pants should recompile Example class but it does NOT write it to the cache.  

It seems like the build_invalidator thinks that the class is in the cache, and when it isn't it gets recompiled but nothing gets pushed to cache.  I believe the class should be written to the cache.  Running pants invalidate between switching branches fixes the problem.

issue_title: Support for resources dependent on stages created by SAM
issue_body:
When using CloudFormation resources that depend on a stage being deployed (such as ApiKey and UsagePlan), it seems that the only way to fill in the stage resource value of the DependsOn attribute is to launch the CF template without any of the dependent resources and get the logical ID from the deployed Resources tab (if deploying from the console). I don't see any documentation for the logical ID naming convention of stages deployed through SAM, which is why I ran the template first to find it.

Could there be some documentation on the naming convention or a way to explicitly define/export the logical ID of stages in a SAM template so that dependent resources can use them?

issue_title: Consider adding default Runtime for all Functions
issue_body:
Currently, we have to explicitly set Runtime property for each Function resource, which makes the template unnecessarily verbose. Since most functions will likely be located in the same file (at least for a single resource) or a set of dependent modules, we can assume they will all run under the same runtime. Moreover, it would be nice to avoid hard-coding the runtime version into the template and specify it as a parameter instead:
```
Parameters:
  Runtime:
    Type: String
    Default: nodejs4.3
    AllowedValues:
      - nodejs
      - nodejs4.3
      - java8
      - python2.7
    ...
```
or even perhaps
```
Parameters:
  Runtime:
    Type: AWS::Lambda::Function::Runtime
    Default: nodejs4.3
    ...
```
for an ultimate conciseness. Or even better (though partisan), define nodejs4.3 as the default value of Runtime in the spec. In the latter case, one should still be able to easily specify a different _global_ runtime using syntax similar to the above.

issue_title: Users with a Facility/Class Coach Role should be redirected to the Coach page after logon
issue_labels: [{'id': 703587154, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/learningequality/kolibri/labels/TAG:%20beginner%20issue', 'name': 'TAG: beginner issue', 'color': 'c1e23b', 'default': False}, {'id': 492816719, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/learningequality/kolibri/labels/TASK:%20ux%20update', 'name': 'TASK: ux update', 'color': '72486f', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
### Observed behavior
If you are a Facility/Class Coach, you are sent to the "Learn" page after logging on. To get to the Coach pages, you need to navigate there via the side navigation.

This behavior was brought up in at least two different QA sessions.

### Expected behavior

More likely than not, if you are a Coach, you are logging into the app to view Coach tools (reports, exams, etc.). It would be nice if the Coach was sent to coach tools after logging in.

### User-facing consequences

Coach users save some extra clicking around.

<!--
Instructions:
 * Fill out the sections below, replace …'s with information about your issue
 * Use the 'preview' function above this text box to verify formatting before submitting
-->

### Observed behavior
<!--
Description of the behavior that was observed, including screenshots or other references when applicable
-->

When downloading content on an unreliable connection (i.e. most of our user base, and me last week) it fails frequently (generally due to timeouts). Starting it up again means closing the error message, and doing a minimum of 5 clicks or so (and pageloads) to start it up again. It's even worse when you selected a granular set of items to import, because selecting them all over again is a huge pain, especially when you might be on a connection that's only loading a couple of items every 5 mins before timing out again.

### Expected behavior
<!--
Description of what behavior was expected but did not occur
-->

When it fails, it should at the very least give you two options: Retry, and Cancel. "Cancel" is what we have now. "Retry" should restart the same import process from scratch (with the same items selected), as it will skip anything that it already has. Even better would be to have it auto-trigger the "Retry" button after a few minutes (could be initiated client-side, as long as the browser is still open) to increase chances of downloading desired content while unattended.

### User-facing consequences
<!--
Implications and real-world consequences for learners, coaches, admins, and other users of the application
-->

On flaky connections, loading content becomes a huge headache that consumes one's entire existence (checking constantly, clicking constantly). Costs may be increased, as overnight bandwidth is often cheaper.

### Context
<!--
Tell us about your environment, including:
 * Kolibri version
 * Operating system
 * Browser
-->

Kolibri 0.7.1 on Windows in Kakuma, Kenya.

### Observed behavior

when enrolling users, the modal doesn't respond to 'enter'. you need tab down to the button:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2367265/36570477-1271f822-17e7-11e8-8d70-066381381da5.png)


### Expected behavior

default modal action triggered with 'enter'

### User-facing consequences

slightly slower for keyboard users

### Context

K07

issue_title: Can we include the license file in the source release tarball ?
issue_body:

issue_title: Extraneous characters display in replay possibly introduced by wget

I have encountered an issue where extraneous characters are being displayed for simple WARCs in replay. For example, https://github.com/oduwsdl/ipwb/blob/master/ipwb/samples/warcs/froggie.warc.gz shows extraneous 'ac' characters before and '0' after the content. A few requests related to ExaBGP logging.

 * It should be possible to set the facility for syslog output.
 * The log level is generally best determined by the application itself and not set as  global value for all log messages by the user.
 * The format of messages sent to syslog is seemingly incorrect or suboptimal.

This last point needs some clarification.  For instance, when an update arrives, my syslog gets something like this:

  Fri, 27 Feb 2015 16:25:58 | INFO     | 18299  | routes        | peer 192.0.2.1 ASN 64496   << UPDATE (2)

Note, this time stamp and the remainder of the message is after the syslog prepends a time stamp and system name.  It is as if the message to the logger device isn't be properly presented.  It would also be best to present a single program name (e.g. exabgp) and then simply have a standard format so that "routes", "reactor" or whatever can be easily identified as part of that well formed message.  In this specific example, it might also be helpful to identify the local peering information, in case there is more than one local listener or distinct local peering information of a set so you know what session this is associated.with.

The only place I see an openlog() method is in etc/exabgp/processes/syslog-1.py with the ExaBGP ident, but I don't see that ident in any logs.

Is the idea that those helper scripts should be used to handle proper syslog messaging?  That seems a little hacky if so.  :-)

It might be worth noting that dev/configuration/first.exa and dev/configuration/first.exa both refer to a a syslog-2.py helper script, but that script doesn't exist.

John

issue_title: _stream_helper returns invalid JSON or cannot decode
issue_body:
**docker-py**
1.8.0

**python**
3.5.0

**docker**
Version 1.11.1-beta10 (build: 6662)

**OS**
Mac OS X 10.11 Beta 15A279b

- - -

While using https://github.com/6si/shipwright, I found that invalid JSON was being returned by `docker-py`. According to the docs, the output of `client.build` should be an iterable of valid JSON objects encoded as strings (or the JSON objects decoded if `decode=True` is passed). e.g.

```
'{"stream":"Step 1 : FROM ubuntu:xenial\\n"}'
```

However, I have found while using the setup listed above, that it is returning strings like:

```
'{"stream":"Step 1 : FROM ubuntu:xenial\\n"}\r\n{"stream":"Step 2 : COPY foo\\n"}\r\n'
```

(Note the trailing CRLFs as well, differs from the documentation)

I suspect this issue is being triggered by the Docker side of things, it looks like the chunks in the chunked response used to align exactly with JSON objects, but now no longer do, although I'm not familiar enough with the Docker API to know if this is correct.

I also suspect I reached this issue because of the pre-release Docker installation, however I see that `docker-py` doesn't list compatible Docker versions, which makes it difficult to know if this is a bug or unimplemented functionality.

The entirety of Python 3.4's ssl module was [backported into Python 2.7.9](https://www.python.org/downloads/release/python-279/).  I believe this means that docker-py's requirement condition (< 3.5) on backports.ssl_match_hostname is slightly inaccurate.  A Python 2.7.9 user doesn't need the backports.ssl_match_hostname dependency.

* https://github.com/docker/docker-py/blob/6a16edee3e7110e0439ef067e5c11db51bd4371a/requirements.txt#L4
* https://github.com/docker/docker-py/blob/6a16edee3e7110e0439ef067e5c11db51bd4371a/setup.py#L23

Unfortunately, the main code that imports match_hostname is doing an import based on the Python version.  It would be more sane to use a try except like the test suite does.

* https://github.com/docker/docker-py/blob/6a16edee3e7110e0439ef067e5c11db51bd4371a/docker/ssladapter.py#L20-L21
* https://github.com/docker/docker-py/blob/6a16edee3e7110e0439ef067e5c11db51bd4371a/tests/unit/ssladapter_test.py#L4-L11

Please update docker-py such that backports.ssl_match_hostname isn't installed on systems that don't need it (Python 2.7.9+).

P.S. On a similar note, should the PY3 minimum be 3.4, not 3.5?

issue_title: AllenNLP's data piece could be more pythonic
issue_body:
allennlp's data API has become pretty stable and effective. However, it's still a bit unwieldy  to manipulate individual `Fields` and `Instances`. There are a few things we could do to make them easier to use for a newcomer:

#### 1. Manipulating `Fields` in `Instances`. `Instance` could inherit from `MutableMapping`. (so could `MetadataField`, in the same vein.)
```
# Currently:
specific_field = instance.fields["field_name"]

# Ideal:
specific_field = instance["field_name"]
"field_name" in instance
>>> True
```

#### 2. Iterating over `SequenceFields` and `ListFields` using `Field.__iter__`:

```
# Before
fields = instance.fields
tokens = [t.text for t in fields['tokens'].tokens]
assert tokens == ["Mali", "government", "officials", "say", "the", "woman", "'s",
                      "confession", "was", "forced", "."]

# After
token_field = instances["tokens"]
assert token_field == ["Mali", "government", "officials", "say", "the", "woman", "'s",
                      "confession", "was", "forced", "."]
```

#### 3. Representing `Index` and `SpanFields` as their values for equality (`Field.__eq__`):
```
# Before
assert instance.fields["span_start"].sequence_index == 102
assert (instance.fields["span"].span_start, instance.fields["span"].span_end) == (102, 109)

# After
assert instance["span_start"] == 102
assert instance["span"] == (102, 109)
```

issue_title: Health checks persist after successful deployment
issue_body: I enabled health checks with `deis config:set HEALTHCHECK_URL=/_health` for an app and once a deploy successfully completes (the health check endpoint returns a 200 response), I am still seeing periodic (10s) requests on `/_health` from `Go-http-client/1.1`. Aren't health checks supposed to stop once one of them passed?

``` 03:37:55 (develop) /media/oliver/BACKUP/Projects/wallz$ deis keys:add Found the following SSH public keys: 1) id_rsa.pub oliver@pfmedia.dk 0) Enter path to pubfile (or use keys:add ) Which would you like to use with Deis? 1 Uploading oliver@pfmedia.dk to Deis...500 INTERNAL SERVER ERROR

Server Error (500)

``` What could be causing this? I recently upgraded to 1.5.0, and I can push updates my apps and so on. This is on a new computer. I am logged in, also.

issue_title: Updating an endpoint with --myproxy-server or --oauth-server should probably clear the other one
issue_body: Today, if you have an endpoint with `oauth_server` set, which Transfer prefers over `myproxy_server`, it's not possible to get the endpoint back to a MyProxy setup using `--myproxy-server` because the `oauth_server` value will be left set. Further, it's not possible to clear it explicitly using something like `--myproxy-server=''` or `--no-myproxy-server`.

issue_title: Add `prefill_named_grant` support to NativeApp grant flow
issue_body:
Per globus/globus-cli#176 , there's an option to prefill the named grant label, `prefill_named_grant`. It's probably a string.

We need to support this so that the CLI and similar applications can use it.

Not considering this blocked on documentation.

Specifically, now that we're using `pyjwt` and (therefore) `cryptography`, we should consider making that dependency a normal requirement.
`globus-sdk[jwt]` would continue to exist as an empty extra for compatibility.

One of our key questions is whether or not we can trust AppVeyor testing to validate that Windows installs work. What version of Windows does AppVeyor run? If we can't trust it, how will we test for Windows? (If we can't trust it, doesn't that undermine all of our testing on that platform anyway?)

issue_title: Change to use `master` as primary branch.
issue_body:
### What is wrong

py-rlp uses `develop` as it's primary development branch.  This is inline with all of the *old* ethereum python codebases, but incongruent with all of the *newer* python codebases.

### How can it be fixed

I propose we change the primary branch to be `master`.

### What is wrong?

There is (probably) a lot of duplicated logic across services, which is why `p2p.BaseService` was written.

### How can it be fixed

Consider whether anything in `trinity.rpc.ipc` would benefit from using `BaseService`, especially `IPCServer`.

In the near future, and in preparation for Anaconda 4.3, we will be offering a miniconda installer that installs conda into a *private env*, while leaving the *default user env* empty except for symlinks of conda's executables into the default user env.

Private named environments are generally useful for any "top-level" application that the prototypical user is likely to only install once on the system.  Examples could include Anaconda Navigator and Spyder.  Not generalizing the concept for other packages to make use of would be naive and negligent.

The convention for a private application environment will be the application's name prefixed and suffixed by an underscore. This distinguishes a private environment from the established use case of an environment name prefixed (only, not suffixed) by underscore, which allows conda to be installed in the environment without complaint.

An application may request that it be installed in a private named environment by indicating so in [non-index package metadata](https://github.com/conda/conda-build/pull/1539).  The request will be respected for conda create, install, update, and remove commands *without* specification of the `--name` or `--prefix` command-line flags.

Private environment preference will only be respected for packages explicitly requested by the user.  Packages installed as dependencies of a user-requested package will not be installed in private environments, but rather the private environment of the user-requested package.

When a package specifies a preferred private environment, it need not be a private environment for itself.  It can instead be the private environment of another package.  For example, conda-build can indicate that its preferred private environment is the conda private environment.

When a private environment request is respected, the executable-marked paths in the private prefix's `bin/` directory will be symlinked into the default user environment.  A package may limit the executables being symlinked by providing an explicit list of desired paths to symlink in the package's non-index metadata.

The issue https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/3074 discusses the more-limited conda-specific implementation.

Installing Miniconda requires approving a license.  On Linux using the shell script, this requires scrolling through the license, pressing Enter multiple times to get to the bottom of the license, at which point the following message is displayed.

    Do you approve the license terms? [yes|no]
    [no] >>>>

Having this default to 'no' seems like a workflow issue. The user is pressing Enter multiple times in rapid succession to get to the end of the license, and then accidentally choosing the default option which ends the process.  The only way to actually accept the license is to carefully, slowly, painstakingly press Enter for each line of the license so you don't accidentally accept the 'no' option.

I think a better approach would be to eliminate the default option.  Something like this.

    Do you approve the license terms? [yes|no]
    >>>>

If the user presses Enter, the message simply repeats, or maybe gives a more explicit message.

    You must type 'yes' to accept the license terms or 'no' to stop the installation [yes|no]
    >>>>

issue_title: Conda packages should contain authoritative dependency information
issue_body:
A packages `meta.yaml` or its equivalent should be embedded into every package.  I should be able to look at a package tarball and find out *exactly* what its dependencies are.  Right now that isn't the case.  In fact the situation is (I would argue) worse than that because it *appears* that the dependencies are embedded, but what is in there is not, in fact, the "real" details of the package dependency.  I still don't understand what the `requires` and `info.json` files contain, but I'm told that the definitive dependencies live elsewhere in a `repodata.json` file that is managed on the package server but not (easily? at all?) available to the conda user who has fetched the package.

A concrete discussion of this can be found here with lots of examples:

https://github.com/ContinuumIO/anaconda-issues/issues/563

I'm a memory-constrained VM (DigitalOcean 512 MB droplet, although ~300 MB are used by other processes). When I run `conda env create` with the following `environment.yml` file:

name: test

issue_title: keeping track of installed binstar packages
issue_body:
As far as I can tell, conda doesn't keep track of the binstar channel that package came from. But, I'm pretty new to conda/binstar, so I may be missing something... For example:

```
conda install --channel https://conda.binstar.org/pelson cartopy
conda list cartopy
```
Results in:
```
cartopy                   0.11.0               np18py27_0
```
But running `conda info cartopy` results in:

```
Error: No packages found in current osx-64 channels matching: cartopy
```

Is there not local metadata for packages installed from binstar?

One of the reasons conda is great is to be able to replicate environments on different machines with `conda list -e > envJoe.txt` so that someone else can run `conda create -n joes_env --file envJoe.txt` However, there are a lot of packages out there that aren't installed from the default channel. Also, some packages have loads of versions on binstar, so it's easy to forget where one installed long ago came from. 

Since conda is aware of packages installed with `pip`, maybe an okay solution is `conda list -e --only-pip` or `conda list -e --only-binstar` so all packages are more easily tracked and shared?

Somewhat related to #919

issue_title: No differentiation between 'local' installtions and 'file://' installation
issue_body:
With conda 4.1.2, and packages installed from `file://` channels,
`conda list --explicit` outputs invalid urls:

    file:///data/packages/conda/linux-64pyside-1.2.4-py34_0.tar.bz2

Notice that there is no '/' between `linux-64` and `pyside`.
The reason is this fix:
https://github.com/conda/conda/commit/1b30ba65b004c97957bf383a8dbb07e56d2f5f0e
Which was intended to fix a 'local' installation, but inadvertently handles `file://` as well.
This is related to #2781, which is also the result of this kind of mix-up.

I think the only robust solution is to add metadata to `local` installations.
CC @mcg1969

The commit c37c00b breaks the [sqlalchemy.engine.reflexion.Introspector ](http://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/latest/core/reflection.html?highlight=reflection#fine-grained-reflection-with-inspector) API. See the following snippet to test it:

```python
import sqlalchemy as sql
from sqlalchemy.engine import reflection

# Connect to the database
engine = sql.create_engine("cockroachdb://root:****@localhost:26257/test_reflect",
                           # echo=True # debug
                           )
meta = sql.MetaData(bind=engine)

# Make sure there are no tables lying around
meta.reflect(); meta.drop_all(); meta.clear()

mytable = sql.Table(
    'mytable', meta,
    sql.Column('id', sql.Integer, primary_key=True, nullable=False),
    sql.Column('fkey', sql.Integer, nullable=False))
mytable.create()

# Test Introspector API
insp = reflection.Inspector.from_engine(meta.bind)
assert(insp.get_primary_keys(mytable)
       == ['id'])
assert(insp.get_indexes(mytable)
       == [{'unique': True, 'name': 'primary', 'column_names': ['id']}])
```

Methods `get_primary_keys` and `get_indexes` failed with:
```
sqlalchemy.exc.ProgrammingError: (psycopg2.ProgrammingError) can't adapt type 'Table' [SQL: '\nSELECT index_name, column_name, (not non_unique::bool) as unique, implicit::bool as implicit\nFROM information_schema.statistics\nWHERE table_schema = %s AND table_name = %s\n        '] [parameters: (u'public', Table('mytable', MetaData(bind=Engine(cockroachdb://root:***@l...)), Column('id', Integer(), table=<mytable>, primary_key=True, nullable=False), Column('fkey', Integer(), table=<mytable>, nullable=False), schema=None))] 
```

Whereas, the same snippet works with the previous commit, i.e.:
```
pip install git+https://github.com/cockroachdb/cockroachdb-python.git@91584060dae2c00ce5ce27a61e5ee47ad6e2b3ba#egg=cockroachdb
```

Tested with CockroachDB v2.0.1 and SQLAlchemy v1.2.10.

issue_title: Fast reindex via parallel scan (sliced scroll) and parallel_bulk

issue_body:
According to the documentation, the `Elasticsearch.index()` method accepts a (string) parameter `timeout` that defines the maximum time to wait for an operation, consisting of a string like `Tu`, where`T` is the time and `u` is the unit (e.g., "30s"). However, when I set this parameter to "30s", I still get:

> ConnectionTimeout: ConnectionTimeout caused by - ReadTimeoutError(HTTPConnectionPool(host=u'172.17.0.40', port=9200): Read timed out. (read timeout=10))

The timeout was still the default value (10 seconds)...

Looking into the code, it seems that `timeout` is just ignored, and that `request_timeout` is used instead, and that it should have a numeric value (not string). Passing `request_timeout` to the `Elasticsearch.index()` method works already, so I think it's just a matter of documentation.

issue_title: Trusted certs pulled over insecure channel
issue_body:
Currently certifi is pulling the ca-bundle over a non-TLS protected channel (http), which means that anyone between the host and ci.kennethreitz.org can inject or completely modify the ca certificates bundle to inject their own trusted ca, allowing them to man-in-the-middle tls traffic between python clients and any site they connect to. 

To address this, the ca bundle should be hosted on a site protected by TLS, and https://github.com/certifi/python-certifi/blob/master/tasks.py#L5 should be updated to reflect the new endpoint.

issue_title: Missing option to set Access-Control-Allow-Headers option
issue_body:
The Jupyter notebook module has the option `--allow_origin` which allows you to set the `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` header.  However, the `Access-Control-Allow-Headers` header is missing.  This results in the `X-XSRFToken` header being rejected for XHR requests, meaning that connecting from a notebook frontend hosted on another server still does not work.

There should be an option to enable the `X-XSRFToken` header under `Access-Control-Allow-Headers`.

issue_title: cp modules cannot be updated / reupped
issue_body:
Modules with cp cannot be updated with peru sync even if the source files have changed.

Not sure if this is intended or not.

To reproduce:

Create `peru.yaml`:

```
$ cat peru.yaml 
imports:
  m: dst/

cp module m:
  path: src/
$ cat src/file
a
$ peru sync
$ cat dst/file 
a
```

Change the file then sync, no change.

```
$ echo b > src/file
$ peru sync
$ cat dst/file
a
```

Clean then sync, still uses initial file.

```
$ peru clean
$ cat dst/file
cat: dst/file: No such file or directory
$ peru sync
$ cat dst/file
a
```

Running reup on the module does not work because cp does not support reup.

issue_title: toggle_state default for active is error-prone
issue_body:
`toggle_state` takes an optional `active` param that lets you specify whether the state should be on or off, instead of it being switched from its previous value.  Having a default value of `None` is problematic because `None` can easily be mistaken for a "truthy" `False` when dealing with relation data, which leads to the state being unexpectedly set.  For example, the following is wrong:

```python
conv.toggle_state('{relation_name}.feature_x',
                  active=conv.get_remote('feature_x_available'))
```

This can also be more subtle because `None and True` and `None and False` both return `None` instead of `False` as one might expect.

Relevant output
https://travis-ci.org/xpdAcq/xpdAn/jobs/263914210#L1249

`db.fill_event` called [here](https://github.com/xpdAcq/xpdAn/blob/aa4b7f42d8b39bcafccff330a95206dc6e6387b4/xpdan/callbacks_core.py#L159)

Large input files cause failure by using more memory than expected during the sort step.

add:

`'--limitBAMsortRAM', '42950000000',` to STAR, which is 40GiB -- the current memory allocation currently assigned to STAR.

issue_title: Customize how entity objects are stringified
issue_body:
When a [`nailgun.entity_mixins.Entity`](http://nailgun.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api/nailgun.html#nailgun.entity_mixins.Entity) object is stringified, little useful information is provided. The full dotted path to the object's type is provided, in addition to its memory address. However, as is clear from the example below, that's not terribly useful, and it's ugly to boot:

```python
>>> location = Location().create()
>>> location.get_values()
{
    'compute_resource': [],
    'config_template': [<nailgun.entities.ConfigTemplate at 0x107c0be90>,
    <nailgun.entities.ConfigTemplate at 0x107c0b910>,
    <nailgun.entities.ConfigTemplate at 0x107c16050>,
    <nailgun.entities.ConfigTemplate at 0x107c16310>,
    <nailgun.entities.ConfigTemplate at 0x107c165d0>,
    <nailgun.entities.ConfigTemplate at 0x107c16890>,
    <nailgun.entities.ConfigTemplate at 0x107c16b50>,
    <nailgun.entities.ConfigTemplate at 0x107c16e10>,
    <nailgun.entities.ConfigTemplate at 0x107c19110>,
    <nailgun.entities.ConfigTemplate at 0x107c193d0>,
    <nailgun.entities.ConfigTemplate at 0x107c19690>,
    <nailgun.entities.ConfigTemplate at 0x107c19950>,
    <nailgun.entities.ConfigTemplate at 0x107c19c10>],
    'description': None,
    'domain': [],
    'environment': [],
    'hostgroup': [],
    'id': 18,
    'media': [],
    'name': u'\ufc8d\u1c73\uc3a5\u0564\ud17e\u7f1a\u55a2\ub819\u040f\uc88b\u569f\u478c\uadc5\uc8c0\ubeb7\u1565\u13c4\ucd9f\u588d\u4258\u42d3\ub4f5\u82e9\u3153\ub04b',
    'organization': [],
    'smart_proxy': [],
    'subnet': [],
    'user': [],
}
```

The process of stringifying entity objects should be customized. Something like this would be more useful:

    <ConfigTemplate 85>

This could backfire - not all entities have IDs. Perhaps this stringification method could try `self.id`, then `self.uuid`, then fall back to the memory address of the object?

issue_title: fields.Method result should be able to be wrapped into other field types

issue_body:
I ran into an issue today where serializing was really slow. Turns out the reason is we we were serializing 200 items 8-9  times each because they appeared in different parts of the code.

I ended up doing something like:

```python
schema = MySchema()
serialized_cache = {}
for item in items:
    if item.item_id not in serialized_cache.keys():
        serialized_cache[item.item_id] = schema.dump(item).data

...later
result = [serialized_cache[item.item_id] for item in items_over_here]

...later
result = [serialized_cache[item.item_id] for item in items_somewhere_else]
```

Code above is not exactly correct but gives the right idea.

Does marshmallow help us do this in some way? I don't necessarily want to suggest that it start doing caching since it's hard to do right and hard to do in a generic way, but it would be great if we could hook into the schema in a way that this was supported on a per schema / schema instance / dump call basis.

Thoughts?

issue_title: MPS object detector should recycle persistent MPSImage instances
issue_body: In the steady state, the object detector should not allocate any new `MPSImage` instances. Any image used only within each batch's command buffer should be a `MPSTemporaryImage`. The result images used for the output of inference or the loss from training should be recycled across batches, for example by setting an appropriate `destinationImageAllocator` on the `MPSNNGraph` that maintains a list of free allocations.

issue_title: Use MLCustomModel to store/return images for ImageSimilarity Core ML model
issue_labels: [{'id': 774366891, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw3NzQzNjY4OTE=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/apple/turicreate/labels/Core%20ML', 'name': 'Core ML', 'color': 'b3a9f2', 'default': False}, {'id': 782185156, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw3ODIxODUxNTY=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/apple/turicreate/labels/feature%20request', 'name': 'feature request', 'color': 'b602f2', 'default': False}, {'id': 962944466, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw5NjI5NDQ0NjY=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/apple/turicreate/labels/image%20similarity', 'name': 'image similarity', 'color': 'd0fcae', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
See discussion on #242.

The image similarity model in Turi Create returns similar images (from the training set), along with probabilities. The exported Core ML model currently changes the interface, returning only probabilities for each index from the training set, and requires the user to store and retrieve the images separately. With the `MLCustomModel` interface now in Core ML, we could in theory return the images directly (by storing them in the Core ML custom model data), giving the same interface in Core ML as in Turi Create.

Update The `Image Similarity Query` documentation to include the information that query allows an arbitrary image as an input:

- https://github.com/apple/turicreate/blob/master/userguide/image_similarity/README.md

issue_title: warning: field 'm_posix_timestamp_high' will be initialized after field 'm_posix_timestamp_low' [-Wreorder]
issue_body:
We cannot check for warnings in our header files and enable -Werror in part because some header files define constructors that attempt to initialize data members out of order:
```
/Users/jong/Source/turicreate/src/flexible_type/flexible_type_base_types.hpp:99:27: warning: 
      field 'm_posix_timestamp_high' will be initialized after field
      'm_posix_timestamp_low' [-Wreorder]
  inline flex_date_time():m_posix_timestamp_high(0), m_posix_timestamp_low(0),
                          ^
```
We should always write member initializer lists in the actual order of initialization, then enable -Wreorder.

issue_title: How to read the interpretation of a bound variable in 'exists' quantifier assertion.md
issue_body:
When I learn the usage of SMT2 by rise4fun.com/z3/tutorial, I was beset by a problem:
`how to eval the interpretation of a bound variable in 'exists' quantifier assertion from a sat model.`

I want to use an example from the tutorial to explain my problem:

In the tutorial, there is a saying: `The command eval evaluates an expression in the last model produced by Z3. It is essentially executing the "function program" produced by Z3.`

So I try to modify the example code following (just change forall  ->  exists), and want to get the value of x in 'exists' quantifier after get-model operation, I find no way to do this. The reason is obvious that the scope of x is only in the body of 'exists' quantifier. But as we all know, Z3 produces an assignment of x in the produced sat model actually, and that is what I want to read.

Can anyone help me?

![2015-08-12 4 20 43](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/8719521/9219577/76c11036-410e-11e5-83aa-adfd68340d74.png)

![2015-08-12 4 21 15](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/8719521/9219593/8f45c80e-410e-11e5-831e-502129526bc9.png)

Page 67 of http://smtlib.cs.uiowa.edu/papers/smt-lib-reference-v2.6-draft-3.pdf says:

> `(echo s)` where s is a string literal, simply prints back s as is—including the surrounding
> double-quotes.

Strictly speaking, z3 is violating this as it does not print the surrounding quotes. I think the motivation is that the output of all commands are valid s-expressions, and putting the quotes would ensure this invariant is always held.

Perhaps not the most important detail, but would be good to be compliant in this case as well.

I should like to suggest a new Z3 tactic that partitions a given SMT-problem (CSP) into minimal independent components. 

The partition algorithm works on the primal constraint graph. 
(1) If two variables appear together in a constraint, they end up in the same CSP-component. 
(2) A constraint that has a variable in one of the CSP-components, ends up in that component. 

The partition tactic should be a splitting tactic like split-clause. The constraints in each component should be combined into a separate goal. 

A tactical such as then could be used to serially solve each of the component-CSPs. 

A tactic such as par-then could be used to solve the component-CSPs in parallel. 
It would be very useful if one could limit the number of threads used by par-then.

Z3 generates invalid models for some queries in the `QF_UFIDL` logic when using the decision procedure based on Bellman-Ford. The model's interpretation of a function maps the same element of the domain to several different values. Below is the smallest example that I could find to demonstrate this behavior.

```lisp
(set-option :auto-config false)
(set-option :smt.arith.solver 1)
(set-option :model_validate true)

(declare-fun f (Int) Int)
(declare-fun g (Int) Int)

; g inverts f over [0, 4)
(assert (= 0 (g (f 0))))
(assert (= 1 (g (f 1))))
(assert (= 2 (g (f 2))))
(assert (= 3 (g (f 3))))

; Bound the range of g to [0, 4) over the domain [0, 4)
(assert (<= 0 (g 0)))
(assert (<= 0 (g 1)))
(assert (<= 0 (g 2)))
(assert (<= 0 (g 3)))
(assert (< (g 0) 4))
(assert (< (g 1) 4))
(assert (< (g 2) 4))
(assert (< (g 3) 4))

; f over [0, 2) is bounded to [2, 4)
(assert (<= 2 (f 0)))
(assert (<= 2 (f 1)))
(assert (< (f 0) 4))

    def call_wrapper(*args, **kwargs):

            return api_call_method(*args, **kwargs)

issue_body:
Related issue:
https://pulp.plan.io/issues/2925

When a pull request comes in, most checks are done automatically by Travis. It's great! However, there's a couple things I do that either cannot be automated or are not automated. Three come to mind:

* For one, I *never* use the green "merge pull request" button. When you click that button, GitHub performs a non-fast-forward merge, even if the pull request is rebasd onto HEAD. That's silly, and it makes quite a few things unnecessarily hard. It makes reverting commits hard, it makes `git bisect` hard, it makes browsing the commit history hard - yeesh.
* I always rebase commits before merging them. Not everyone has the know-how to do this, and it's nice that I can do this for people.
* I check commit messages, both for formatting and content. Regarding formatting, I strongly like having a short headline, and a body with hard-wrapped text. This makes browsing the commit history with `git log`, `git show` and other such tools much easier. Regarding content, I strongly like having a descriptive message that states what's being done, why, and what the effect is on test suite results.

* NGINX server block treatment sync
  * Installing certificates
    * If >= 1 matching sslish block, add cert info to all matching sslish blocks
    * Else if >= 1 matching non-sslish block, make all matching non-sslish blocks sslish, and add cert info
    * For 0.9.0, if there are no matching blocks, error out and say “sorry you need to give me a relevant server block before I can install a cert for you”.
  * Redirect enhancement
    * At redirect time, there will be at least one matching sslish block.
    * If there are only matching sslish blocks, add listen on 80 and if statement redirect to all matching sslish blocks.
    * Else if there are both matching sslish and matching plaintextish blocks, add the if statement redirect to all matching plaintextish blocks.

issue_title: Wrong byte order in patient data
issue_body:
When trying to load patient data, mentioned here: https://neptun.phys.au.dk/~bassler/TRiP/README.md:

> TST000 : Anonymous patient, not for distribution.

bytes are swapped:

```
→ cat tests/bug.py                        
from pytrip import CtxCube

c = CtxCube()
c.read("tests/TST000/TST000000.ctx")

print("shape", c.cube.shape)
print("element [0][0][0]", c.cube[0][0][0])
print("min value", c.cube.min())
print("max value", c.cube.max())

# grzanka at gajewski-dell in ~/PycharmProjects/pytrip on git:master ✖︎ [18:05:52]
→ PYTHONPATH=. python3 tests/bug.py
shape (102, 256, 256)
element [0][0][0] 6396
min value -32768
max value 32767
```

Inspecting file with hexeditor:
```
# grzanka at gajewski-dell in ~/PycharmProjects/pytrip on git:master ✖︎ [18:05:56]
→ hexdump tests/TST000/TST000000.ctx -n 20
0000000 18fc 18fc 18fc 18fc 18fc 18fc 18fc 18fc
*
0000014
```
18fc in hex means 6369 in little endian encoding and -1000 in big endian

In header file:
```
→ cat tests/TST000/TST000000.hed | grep byte_order
byte_order vms
```

Byteswapping code in pytrip (https://github.com/pytrip/pytrip/blob/master/pytrip/cube.py#L423) is following:
```
        if self.byte_order == "aix":
            cube = cube.byteswap()
```
It doesn't swap bytes for vms byte order.

Swapping the bytes or changing byte order to aix would solve the problem

issue_title: Problems with Bootstrap 4 formatting hack in helper.FormHelper
issue_body:
In [Cookiecutter Django](https://github.com/pydanny/cookiecutter-django) we've discovered that when using Bootstrap 4, there is a bit of code in Crispy Forms which is causing us a lot of problems. This code is caused us serious problems with all our forms:

```
class FormHelper(DynamicLayoutHandler):
    ...
    if template_pack == 'bootstrap4':
        grid_colum_matcher = re.compile('\w*col-(xs|sm|md|lg|xl)-\d+\w*')
        using_grid_layout = (grid_colum_matcher.match(self.label_class) or
                             grid_colum_matcher.match(self.field_class))
        if using_grid_layout:
            items['using_grid_layout'] = True
```

Our issues with the above approach:

1. Fragile: Hardcoding like this assumes Bootstrap 4's API doesn't change (it does)
2. Unforgiving: Doesn't allow for any variation in template design
3. Really Unforgiving: No way to override this behavior
4. Undocumented: No mention in the documentation, or it's too hard for me to find

Right now our solution is to implement our own hack to get around this behavior, which is not ideal and something we want to fix.

Previously, the VOD URL would be nested within the creator's channel, now it looks like all videos are within a "videos" folder.

For instance, previously, I would use the following command prompt to launch a VOD:

Now, the VOD link would be:

At least according to the links displayed on this creator's content page: https://www.twitch.tv/northernlion/videos/all

It appears there is some behind the scene resolving of URL that were borked with this change in hierarchy, the error I receive when using the URL form the VOD is:

issue_title: Are floats really a good enough representation for trio times?
issue_body:
Trio's timekeeping is concerned entirely with monotonic durations, not absolute wallclock times, so we don't have to worry about a lot of complexities that wallclocks introduces; in principle we just need a way to represent a simple real number. But a `float` is not a real number. As [PEP 564](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0564/) points out, computer clocks commonly have nanosecond precision these days, but using floats to represent time has less-than-nanosecond precision when dealing with time spans of more than 104 days [or 97 days is what I get for the same calculation... weird. Still, the basic principle is certainly sound]. To handle this, PEP 564 adds new timing functions like `time.monotonic_ns` that use nanosecond integers. These are available starting in 3.7.

Does Trio care? It's not clear. `epoll_wait` only has millisecond precision, and our clock on Windows currently has worse-than-millisecond precision (#33), and `GetQueuedCompletionStatusEx` has ms precision. OTOH, `kevent` takes a `struct timespec` which nominally has nanosecond precision, and it's possible to get higher precision on Linux using a `timerfd`.

We should also consider OS scheduling latency (the gap between when we asked the OS to wake us up, and when it actually wakes us up) and trio scheduler latency (the gap between when the OS wakes us up, and when trio has finished fiddling around in its scheduler and started actually running a task).

For regular processes running on untuned desktop systems with default settings, scheduling latency is often in the millisecond to tens of millisecond range, IIRC. (Maybe I should double-check in case this has changed.) OTOH, I know that if you set up Linux right (e.g. running your process at RT priority, and perhaps using the RT kernel), you can easily get OS scheduling accuracy down into the single-digit microsecond range. This isn't Trio's main use case, but it's a real enough case that maybe we don't want to rule it out at the public API level. OTOH, AFAIK we're still talking about thousands of nanoseconds.

Trio scheduling latency: at least on CPython it's completely impossible to get this down below tens of nanoseconds, because literally every operation in CPython takes that long (e.g. on my laptop creating an empty list is ~20 ns, an empty function call is ~70 ns, accessing an object attribute is ~50 ns, etc.). So getting the latency down to 100 ns would be pretty impressive, and the difference between, say, 1 ns and 2 ns precision in our deadlines is definitely not meaningful. Of course, on PyPy potentially things can go faster...  but for comparison, this is a 2.6 GHz chip, so when the CPU is running at nominal speed a single clock cycle is ~0.38 ns, L1 is a few ns away, etc.

How much accuracy do we lose from using floats? It's hard to know exactly because it depends on the actual value of our clock times – floating point means that larger values get lower precision. Normally we use an epoch that's something approximating boot time, and we can expect all values to be within, say, a year or two of zero. There's no particular guarantee of that, though. If our values are at ~1 year, then a float has a resolution of ~4 ns:

```python
In [55]: t = float(365 * 24 * 60 * 60); (np.nextafter(t, float("inf")) - t) * 1e9
Out[55]: 3.725290298461914
```

OTOH, if we use the standard Unix epoch, then a float currently has a resolution of ~240 ns:

```python
In [57]: t = time.time(); (np.nextafter(t, float("inf")) - t) * 1e9
Out[57]: 238.4185791015625
```

and in 2038 the resolution will drop by a factor of two, becoming ~477 ns. Then in 2016 it'll drop to ~1 µs, which will last until 2242. So even if we were using the Unix epoch for some reason, 64-bit floating point would let us set wakeups with µs precision until 2242, and µs precision is about the most we can currently hope for anyway given OS constraints.

Are there any other reasons we might want high precision? It's nice to be able to give things unique timestamps without collisions... but we can't realistically call `current_time()` twice within, say, 100 ns, and an uptime clock retains that much precision for ~15 years. Plus the use of `MockClock` for testing means systems will probably want to be robust against non-advancing time anyway.

What if there's some unforeseen reason why in the future, we decide we need to increase precision, to nanoseconds or, I dunno, picoseconds? I think it should be pretty easy to increase precision: we would want to change the `Clock` interface to use nanoseconds or whatever, but that's straightforward (and we could shim old `Clock` implementations easily enough: `if not hasattr(clock, "current_time_ns"): clock = OldClockToNewClock(clock)`). And the other three primitive places time is exposed are `cancel_scope.deadline`, `current_time`, and `current_effective_deadline`. In each case we could expose `*_ns` variant, and keep the old one as a shim that converts between the old float seconds representation and the new integer nanoseconds (or whatever) representation.

So I think we don't need to worry about this for now, and this can be closed.
The issues in #179, #180, and #181 apply to `test_bash_apps.py` as well

We will need to create a new scalar type to support this.

https://github.com/graphql-python/graphql-core/issues/24

issue_title: An issue with using scHi-C with PHATE
issue_body:
@kmoon3 @scottgigante The problem with using binary contact maps with 1 along the diagonals as input to PHATE(using default parameters) is that a compact(kinda globular) structure is obtained. We do not want that! So I think this points to: first impute the missing values(due to very sparse single cell Hi-C data) by using MAGIC before applying PHATE?

I tried to use PHATE on chromosomes for cell 1 and cell 2 from https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/geo/query/acc.cgi?acc=GSE80280. At the bottom of that page we use GSE80280_RAW.tar custom file.

In that, the RAW results typically lists a number of observed contacts between specific genome positions. From the raw results, the contacts were aggregated into equally spaced bins along the chromosomes. I worked with a bin size of 50 kbp. Then all observed contacts were assigned to their corresponding bins. In the case that multiple contacts fell into the same bin, the duplicate entries were ignored so that a binary contact matrix C_ij was obtained for each chromosome. Hence, C_ij = 1 represents a Hi-C contact between bins i and j, while C_ij = 0 represents the absence of a contact.

An example file for cell-1 chr1 is below:
The first row list the #bins and #contacts
[GSM2219497_cell1_chr1_50kb.txt](https://github.com/KrishnaswamyLab/PHATE/files/2113163/GSM2219497_cell1_chr1_50kb.txt)

After removing bins with no interactions, the binary contact matrix for the same chr1 is:
[contact_cell1_chr1.txt.tar.gz](https://github.com/KrishnaswamyLab/PHATE/files/2113161/contact_cell1_chr1.txt.tar.gz)

Any help would be greatly appreciated!
Thanks,
-Tarak
@tarak77

issue_title: group.members.get only working if result is within first 20
issue_body:
## Description of the problem, including code/CLI snippet

`group.members.get(userid)` only seems to be working if that userid is within first 20 hits.
Run this on any group with more than 20 members:

```python
group = gl.groups.get('some-group')
# iterate over the exhaustive list of members as a generator
for member in group.members.list(as_list=False):
    # so this call should never fail; but it does
    group.members.get(member.id)
```

## Expected Behavior

Ultimately it should call `https://docs.gitlab.com/ce/api/members.html#get-a-member-of-a-group-or-project` with the exact group id and user id.  This would be most efficient.

Alternatively it could call `.list()` in such a way that it is able to iterate over all results. For example `.list(as_list=False)` to force a generator to be returned or `.list(all=True)` to force the exhaustive list to be returned.  But again, this is bad if there is a more efficient API to call as linked to above.

## Actual Behavior

It ends up calling `GetFromListMixin` which ends up calling `http_list` with no arguments which just returns 20 items.  It then compares those id's to the requested one.

## Specifications

  - python-gitlab version: 1.3.0
  - API version you are using (v3/v4): 4

issue_title: Rename DTC to BTS across the entire proejct
issue_body:
#### Issue Checklist:
- [x] Descriptive title
- [x] Completed sections below

### Problem Description
We call the transform template DTC across the board.  We want to rename the terminology of the template to something that presents the idea of what it does clearly.
- Must contain the name Blurr
- Must represent that it is used to define transformations
- Must be TLA (Three Letter Acronym)

### Solution Proposal
- Rename Readme / Documentation
- Rename in code


### Impact on current behavior
- Existing published articles

Some of the operators (e.g. OptimizedRNN, see #149) require several inputs that can be independently present or absent.

Possible solution: allow some of the positional inputs to be 'None' in the graph with inputs represented as empty string in the proto. Schema checker would enforce that they can appear only after the MinArgs provided.

If not objections - I can cut the diff.

ONNX needs an [awesome list](https://github.com/sindresorhus/awesome). All that exists now is a short list of [tools](https://github.com/onnx/onnx#tools), but the real list is already a bit longer.

MXNet already has [one](https://github.com/chinakook/Awesome-MXNet)(with some ONNX stuff on it).

I can open the file `parser.lark`: ```regex grammar : /[a-zØ-öø-ÿ]/ ``` Like this: ```python #! /usr/bin/env python # -*- coding: utf-8 -*- import lark grammar_file_path = "parser.lark" with open( grammar_file_path, "r", encoding='utf-8' ) as file: _parser = lark.Lark( file.read(), start='grammar', parser='lalr' ) ``` But with `Lark.open()`, no good: ```python #! /usr/bin/env python # -*- coding: utf-8 -*- import lark grammar_file_path = "parser.lark" _parser = lark.Lark.open( "parser.lark", start='grammar', parser='lalr' ) ``` -->

``` Traceback (most recent call last): File "d:\lark-parser\lark\lexer.py", line 221, in __init__ lexer = lexer_by_tokens[key] KeyError: frozenset({Token(TERMINAL, '__ANON_0'), 'grammar'}) During handling of the above exception, another exception occurred: Traceback (most recent call last): File "d:\lark-parser\lark\lexer.py", line 179, in __init__ re.compile(t.pattern.to_regexp()) File "F:\Python\lib\re.py", line 233, in compile return _compile(pattern, flags) File "F:\Python\lib\re.py", line 301, in _compile p = sre_compile.compile(pattern, flags) File "F:\Python\lib\sre_compile.py", line 562, in compile p = sre_parse.parse(p, flags) File "F:\Python\lib\sre_parse.py", line 855, in parse p = _parse_sub(source, pattern, flags & SRE_FLAG_VERBOSE, 0) File "F:\Python\lib\sre_parse.py", line 416, in _parse_sub not nested and not items)) File "F:\Python\lib\sre_parse.py", line 553, in _parse raise source.error(msg, len(this) + 1 + len(that)) sre_constants.error: bad character range ˜-Ã at position 5 During handling of the above exception, another exception occurred: Traceback (most recent call last): File "D:\no_ambiguous.py", line 11, in _parser = lark.Lark.open( "parser.lark", start='grammar', parser='lalr' ) File "d:\lark-parser\lark\lark.py", line 206, in open return cls(f, **options) File "d:\lark-parser\lark\lark.py", line 165, in __init__ self.parser = self._build_parser() File "d:\lark-parser\lark\lark.py", line 188, in _build_parser return self.parser_class(self.lexer_conf, parser_conf, options=self.options) File "d:\lark-parser\lark\parser_frontends.py", line 46, in __init__ self.init_contextual_lexer(lexer_conf, parser_conf) File "d:\lark-parser\lark\parser_frontends.py", line 23, in init_contextual_lexer user_callbacks=lexer_conf.callbacks) File "d:\lark-parser\lark\lexer.py", line 225, in __init__ lexer = Lexer(state_tokens, ignore=ignore, user_callbacks=user_callbacks) File "d:\lark-parser\lark\lexer.py", line 181, in __init__ raise LexError("Cannot compile token %s: %s" % (t.name, t.pattern)) NameError: name 'LexError' is not defined ```


Perhaps a encoding parameter for `Lark.open(file_path)` would do the job?

issue_title: [QUESTION] Storing search results in a python object (twint module)
issue_body:
Using the twint module, is there a way to store data in dicts or lists?  

I know the data can be written to csv/json files with twint.run.Search(c), as well as printing to terminal.  

I'm interested though, in creating a dict for example with certain fields, similar to how the documentation shows you how to format csv/json files with certain fields (e.g., id, username).  

Alternatively I guess I could create the csv/json file and then immediately open/read the file, but wondering if there's a way to cut out this middle step.

I just upgraded virtualenv on an ubuntu natty system and am no longer able to make relocatable virtualenvs. Downgrading to 1.7 solves the problem, so this was probably introduced with the fix for --relocatable and python 3 in 1.7.1.

Installing setuptools............done.
Installing pip...............done.
ubuntu@waldzell:~$ virtualenv --relocatable ve

Traceback (most recent call last):
